title: AP U.S. History MCQ Practice Exam 05

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Secondary-source interpretation. A historian argues that the Haudenosaunee Confederacy before sustained European colonization shows that some Native societies used diplomacy, kinship, councils, and alliance-building to manage conflict among distinct nations. Which evidence would best support the historian’s interpretation?

choices.A: Archaeological evidence that maize farming was absent from all northeastern Native communities
choices.B: Spanish records describing silver mines in the Andes
choices.C: Oral traditions and diplomatic records describing council decision-making among allied nations
choices.D: British parliamentary debates over colonial taxation

questions.correctAnswerText: Oral traditions and diplomatic records describing council decision-making among allied nations
questions.explanation: Council decision-making and alliance records directly support the interpretation of Native diplomacy and confederated political authority.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Secondary-source interpretation. A historian argues that the Haudenosaunee Confederacy before sustained European colonization shows that diplomacy, kinship, councils, and alliance-building helped manage conflict among distinct nations. Which broader context best helps explain this system?

choices.A: Many Native societies developed political structures suited to local needs before European colonization.
choices.B: Native peoples depended on English constitutional law before 1492.
choices.C: European monarchs appointed all Native council leaders before colonization.
choices.D: Native confederacies emerged only after the United States Constitution was written.

questions.correctAnswerText: Many Native societies developed political structures suited to local needs before European colonization.
questions.explanation: The interpretation places Native governance in a precontact context rather than treating political organization as a European import.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Secondary-source interpretation. A historian argues that the Haudenosaunee Confederacy before sustained European colonization shows that Native diplomacy and council systems could create durable political alliances among distinct nations. Which argument about precontact North America is best supported?

choices.A: Native political life was too simple to sustain regional alliances.
choices.B: Native peoples had no diplomatic traditions before European contact.
choices.C: Native societies developed varied forms of governance that cannot be reduced to a single model.
choices.D: Native communities were politically unified under one continental government.

questions.correctAnswerText: Native societies developed varied forms of governance that cannot be reduced to a single model.
questions.explanation: The Haudenosaunee example supports an argument for diversity and complexity in Native political organization.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described map, c. 1650–1720. French trading posts appear along the St. Lawrence River and Great Lakes. Dutch and later English posts appear near the Hudson River. Native villages are connected to European posts by canoe routes. Labels note exchanges of furs, metal tools, firearms, cloth, and diplomatic gifts. Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: The fur trade connected Native diplomacy, European competition, and Atlantic commerce.
choices.B: French and Dutch traders avoided contact with Native communities.
choices.C: Fur-trade routes were unrelated to waterways.
choices.D: English colonists controlled all Great Lakes trade before 1650.

questions.correctAnswerText: The fur trade connected Native diplomacy, European competition, and Atlantic commerce.
questions.explanation: The map links trading posts, waterways, Native villages, European powers, goods, and diplomatic exchange.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described map, c. 1650–1720. French trading posts appear along the St. Lawrence River and Great Lakes. Dutch and later English posts appear near the Hudson River. Native villages are connected to European posts by canoe routes. Which geographic factor most directly shaped the trade network shown?

choices.A: Plantation soils along the Chesapeake Bay
choices.B: Navigable rivers, lakes, and portage routes in the Northeast and Great Lakes
choices.C: Desert irrigation canals in the Southwest
choices.D: Mountain passes leading to California gold fields

questions.correctAnswerText: Navigable rivers, lakes, and portage routes in the Northeast and Great Lakes
questions.explanation: The map’s canoe routes and posts along rivers and lakes show how geography shaped fur-trade networks.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described map, c. 1650–1720. French, Dutch, and English trading posts are connected to Native villages by waterways and canoe routes. Labels note exchanges of furs, metal tools, firearms, cloth, and diplomatic gifts. Which broader development is most directly reflected?

choices.A: European empires and Native nations used trade to build alliances and compete for power.
choices.B: European colonization ended Native influence over interior trade.
choices.C: The fur trade replaced all Native subsistence activities with plantation agriculture.
choices.D: Atlantic commerce had no effect on Native-European relations.

questions.correctAnswerText: European empires and Native nations used trade to build alliances and compete for power.
questions.explanation: The exchange of goods and diplomatic gifts shows trade as both economic and political.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described map, c. 1650–1720. French, Dutch, and English posts connect to Native villages through waterways, and exchanges include furs, tools, weapons, cloth, and diplomatic gifts. Which later development grew out of similar imperial and Native competition in North America?

choices.A: The Seven Years’ War and related conflicts over territory and alliances
choices.B: The creation of Medicare and Medicaid
choices.C: The nullification controversy over tariffs
choices.D: The Red Scare after World War I

questions.correctAnswerText: The Seven Years’ War and related conflicts over territory and alliances
questions.explanation: Imperial and Native competition in interior North America helped fuel broader eighteenth-century conflicts.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated petition by free and enslaved Black residents of Massachusetts, 1777, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. “The language of liberty is spoken in every town, yet many who labor here remain held as property. If natural rights are true, they cannot depend on complexion.” The petition’s point of view is best explained by which historical situation?

choices.A: African Americans used Revolutionary ideology to challenge slavery and racial exclusion.
choices.B: Southern states had already abolished slavery before independence.
choices.C: Loyalists demanded that slavery be expanded into New England.
choices.D: The federal government had already guaranteed equal citizenship to all Black Americans.

questions.correctAnswerText: African Americans used Revolutionary ideology to challenge slavery and racial exclusion.
questions.explanation: The petition applies the language of liberty and natural rights to slavery and race.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated petition by free and enslaved Black residents of Massachusetts, 1777, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The petition argues that natural rights “cannot depend on complexion.” Which claim is best supported by the petition?

choices.A: Revolutionary ideals could be used by marginalized groups to expose contradictions in American claims to liberty.
choices.B: Revolutionary ideology had no relationship to slavery debates.
choices.C: Black petitioners rejected the language of natural rights.
choices.D: The Revolution immediately created racial equality throughout the United States.

questions.correctAnswerText: Revolutionary ideals could be used by marginalized groups to expose contradictions in American claims to liberty.
questions.explanation: The petition highlights the contradiction between natural rights rhetoric and racial slavery.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated petition by free and enslaved Black residents of Massachusetts, 1777, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The petition uses Revolutionary language to challenge bondage and racial exclusion. Which later development most directly continued the petitioners’ appeal to national ideals?

choices.A: Abolitionist and civil rights arguments that invoked founding principles against racial injustice
choices.B: The creation of the Second Bank of the United States
choices.C: The adoption of national-origins immigration quotas in the 1920s
choices.D: The expansion of overseas naval bases after 1898

questions.correctAnswerText: Abolitionist and civil rights arguments that invoked founding principles against racial injustice
questions.explanation: Later reformers repeatedly used founding ideals to critique racial inequality.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated petition by free and enslaved Black residents of Massachusetts, 1777, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The petition argues that natural rights cannot depend on complexion. Which argument about the American Revolution is best supported?

choices.A: The Revolution’s language of liberty was contested and reinterpreted by groups excluded from full rights.
choices.B: Revolutionary ideology was limited to foreign policy.
choices.C: Black activists avoided written petitions during the Revolutionary era.
choices.D: The Revolution ended all disputes over slavery and citizenship.

questions.correctAnswerText: The Revolution’s language of liberty was contested and reinterpreted by groups excluded from full rights.
questions.explanation: The petition shows excluded Black residents using Revolutionary language to demand broader rights.

questions.prompt:
Stimulus: Plain-text timeline of early republic neutrality.
1793 | War begins between Britain and revolutionary France; Washington issues a neutrality proclamation.
1794 | U.S. diplomats negotiate with Britain over forts, trade, and maritime conflict.
1797–1798 | U.S. ships face French seizures; public anger rises.
Which conclusion is best supported by the timeline?

choices.A: The early republic avoided all foreign-policy controversy.
choices.B: European wars forced U.S. leaders to balance neutrality, trade, and national security.
choices.C: The United States entered a permanent military alliance with France in 1793.
choices.D: Britain immediately abandoned every western fort after the Revolution.

questions.correctAnswerText: European wars forced U.S. leaders to balance neutrality, trade, and national security.
questions.explanation: The timeline connects European war, neutrality, negotiations with Britain, and French seizures of U.S. ships.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text timeline of early republic neutrality: Washington issued a neutrality proclamation in 1793 after war began between Britain and revolutionary France; U.S. diplomats negotiated with Britain in 1794; U.S. ships faced French seizures in 1797–1798. Which domestic effect followed from these foreign-policy pressures?

choices.A: Party divisions deepened over whether the United States should lean toward Britain or France.
choices.B: Political conflict disappeared because all Americans agreed on neutrality.
choices.C: The Constitution was replaced by the Articles of Confederation.
choices.D: States gained exclusive authority over diplomacy.

questions.correctAnswerText: Party divisions deepened over whether the United States should lean toward Britain or France.
questions.explanation: Foreign-policy crises sharpened Federalist and Democratic-Republican divisions in the 1790s.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text timeline of early republic neutrality: 1793 neutrality proclamation; 1794 negotiations with Britain; 1797–1798 French seizures of U.S. ships. Which challenge facing the new nation is most directly reflected?

choices.A: Defining how an independent republic would protect sovereignty while avoiding destructive foreign entanglements
choices.B: Determining whether to restore monarchy in the United States
choices.C: Ending all transatlantic commerce
choices.D: Deciding whether western territories should permit slavery after the Mexican-American War

questions.correctAnswerText: Defining how an independent republic would protect sovereignty while avoiding destructive foreign entanglements
questions.explanation: Neutrality and maritime conflict raised questions about sovereignty, diplomacy, and national survival.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described map, c. 1820. A line at 36°30′ is drawn across the Louisiana Purchase. Missouri is marked as entering as a slave state, Maine as entering as a free state, and most remaining Louisiana Purchase territory north of the line is labeled closed to slavery. Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: The Missouri Compromise attempted to balance sectional interests while regulating slavery’s expansion.
choices.B: The Missouri Compromise abolished slavery in every state.
choices.C: The Missouri Compromise admitted only southern states to the Union.
choices.D: The Missouri Compromise ended all future conflict over slavery in the territories.

questions.correctAnswerText: The Missouri Compromise attempted to balance sectional interests while regulating slavery’s expansion.
questions.explanation: The map shows Missouri and Maine admitted in balance and a geographic limit on slavery.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described map, c. 1820. Missouri enters as a slave state, Maine enters as a free state, and a 36°30′ line limits slavery in much of the Louisiana Purchase territory. Which broader issue is most directly reflected by the geographic division shown?

choices.A: Whether western expansion would preserve a balance between free and slave states
choices.B: Whether Native nations would control the Atlantic coast
choices.C: Whether New England would become part of Canada
choices.D: Whether the United States should acquire overseas islands

questions.correctAnswerText: Whether western expansion would preserve a balance between free and slave states
questions.explanation: The line and paired admission of Missouri and Maine reflect sectional balancing over slavery.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described map, c. 1820. The 36°30′ line divides much of the Louisiana Purchase by slavery status, while Missouri and Maine are admitted in a sectional balance. Which later development most directly challenged the compromise framework shown?

choices.A: The Kansas-Nebraska Act’s repeal of the Missouri Compromise line
choices.B: The creation of the Environmental Protection Agency
choices.C: The Neutrality Proclamation of 1793
choices.D: The passage of the National Origins Act

questions.correctAnswerText: The Kansas-Nebraska Act’s repeal of the Missouri Compromise line
questions.explanation: The Kansas-Nebraska Act overturned the restriction represented by the 36°30′ line.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described map, c. 1820. Missouri is admitted as a slave state, Maine as a free state, and the 36°30′ line limits slavery in much of the Louisiana Purchase. Which argument is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Political compromise temporarily managed sectional conflict but did not remove its underlying causes.
choices.B: Sectional conflict ended permanently after 1820.
choices.C: Western expansion had no relationship to slavery.
choices.D: The federal government refused to address territorial slavery.

questions.correctAnswerText: Political compromise temporarily managed sectional conflict but did not remove its underlying causes.
questions.explanation: The map shows compromise over slavery’s expansion, but later events revealed continuing conflict.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated Cherokee newspaper editorial, 1820s, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. “We have farms, laws, schools, and a printed paper to speak for our nation. Yet neighbors call our land unused because they desire it. Civilization cannot mean surrender.” The editorial’s purpose is best explained by which historical situation?

choices.A: Cherokee leaders used written law, schooling, and print culture to defend sovereignty against removal pressures.
choices.B: Cherokee leaders demanded immediate admission as a slave state.
choices.C: Federal officials invited all Native nations to control state governments.
choices.D: Southern planters opposed acquiring Native land for cotton cultivation.

questions.correctAnswerText: Cherokee leaders used written law, schooling, and print culture to defend sovereignty against removal pressures.
questions.explanation: The editorial defends Cherokee sovereignty through evidence of farms, laws, schools, and a newspaper.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated Cherokee newspaper editorial, 1820s, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The editorial states that Cherokee people have farms, laws, schools, and a printed paper, yet outsiders call their land unused because they desire it. Which claim is best supported?

choices.A: Native sovereignty arguments could use the language of law, agriculture, and print to challenge settler claims.
choices.B: Cherokee leaders rejected all written communication.
choices.C: Removal pressure disappeared once Native nations adopted written constitutions.
choices.D: White settlers generally accepted Cherokee land rights without conflict.

questions.correctAnswerText: Native sovereignty arguments could use the language of law, agriculture, and print to challenge settler claims.
questions.explanation: The editorial uses evidence of Cherokee institutions to refute claims that their land was “unused.”

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated Cherokee newspaper editorial, 1820s, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The editorial defends Cherokee land, law, schools, and print culture against outsiders who claim the land is unused. Which broader context most directly shaped the conflict?

choices.A: Expansion of cotton agriculture, state pressure, and white settlement in the Southeast
choices.B: Decline of all migration into the southern interior
choices.C: Federal protection of Native land from all state interference
choices.D: U.S. withdrawal from western expansion after the War of 1812

questions.correctAnswerText: Expansion of cotton agriculture, state pressure, and white settlement in the Southeast
questions.explanation: White settlement and cotton expansion increased pressure on Cherokee lands in the Southeast.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated secession convention statement, 1861, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. “Our society rests upon property in labor recognized by our laws. A hostile sectional party now threatens that security through the power of the national government.” Which issue is most directly reflected in the statement?

choices.A: Southern secessionists defended slavery and feared antislavery federal power.
choices.B: Southern secessionists sought to abolish plantation agriculture.
choices.C: Northern Republicans promised immediate racial equality in every state.
choices.D: Southern states seceded primarily to protest railroad regulation.

questions.correctAnswerText: Southern secessionists defended slavery and feared antislavery federal power.
questions.explanation: The statement’s defense of “property in labor” and fear of a hostile party point to slavery and federal authority.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated secession convention statement, 1861, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The speaker describes a “hostile sectional party” threatening property in enslaved labor through national power. Which political development most directly triggered this argument?

choices.A: The election of Abraham Lincoln and the Republican Party’s opposition to slavery’s expansion
choices.B: The ratification of the Articles of Confederation
choices.C: The creation of the Populist Party
choices.D: The passage of the Social Security Act

questions.correctAnswerText: The election of Abraham Lincoln and the Republican Party’s opposition to slavery’s expansion
questions.explanation: Lincoln’s election convinced many southern secessionists that slavery’s expansion was threatened.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated secession convention statement, 1861, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The statement defends “property in labor” and condemns a hostile sectional party. Which broader context best explains the statement?

choices.A: Decades of sectional conflict over slavery, party realignment, and federal authority
choices.B: The decline of slavery as a political issue after 1850
choices.C: The disappearance of plantation agriculture in the Lower South
choices.D: The success of national compromise in ending all sectional division

questions.correctAnswerText: Decades of sectional conflict over slavery, party realignment, and federal authority
questions.explanation: The secession statement emerged from long conflict over slavery, party politics, and national power.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated secession convention statement, 1861, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The statement claims that slavery is protected by southern law and threatened by national power. Which argument about the coming of the Civil War is best supported?

choices.A: Slavery and disputes over federal authority were central to secession.
choices.B: Secession occurred mainly because of immigration restriction.
choices.C: Foreign alliances caused southern states to leave the Union.
choices.D: The conflict had no relationship to the national government.

questions.correctAnswerText: Slavery and disputes over federal authority were central to secession.
questions.explanation: The source explicitly connects secessionist fears to slavery and national political power.

questions.prompt:
Stimulus: Plain-text table of postwar southern labor arrangements.
Feature | Sharecropping pattern, late 1860s–1870s
Land ownership | Usually controlled by white landowners
Labor | Freed families farmed plots for a share of the crop
Credit | Supplies often purchased on credit at high interest
Debt | Many families ended the year owing merchants or landlords
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Sharecropping gave most freed families immediate landownership.
choices.B: Sharecropping combined limited autonomy with economic dependence.
choices.C: Sharecropping ended agricultural production in the South.
choices.D: Sharecropping removed merchants and landlords from rural life.

questions.correctAnswerText: Sharecropping combined limited autonomy with economic dependence.
questions.explanation: Sharecropping allowed family-based labor but kept many freedpeople dependent on landowners, merchants, and debt.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of postwar southern labor arrangements. White landowners usually controlled land, freed families farmed plots for a share of the crop, supplies were often bought on credit at high interest, and many families ended the year in debt. Which development most directly contributed to this system?

choices.A: Freedpeople’s desire for family-based labor combined with lack of land redistribution
choices.B: Federal seizure and permanent redistribution of most plantations to formerly enslaved people
choices.C: The abolition of cotton farming after the Civil War
choices.D: The creation of factory work as the dominant southern occupation

questions.correctAnswerText: Freedpeople’s desire for family-based labor combined with lack of land redistribution
questions.explanation: Sharecropping developed from freedpeople’s desire for autonomy and the persistence of white landownership.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of postwar southern labor arrangements. The table shows land controlled by white landowners, freed families farming for crop shares, high-interest credit, and persistent debt. Which political issue was connected to this labor system?

choices.A: Whether the postwar South would protect freedpeople’s economic independence and civil rights
choices.B: Whether Britain should regulate colonial shipping
choices.C: Whether the United States should create overseas colonies
choices.D: Whether the 1920s quota system should favor northern Europe

questions.correctAnswerText: Whether the postwar South would protect freedpeople’s economic independence and civil rights
questions.explanation: Sharecropping was tied to broader Reconstruction debates over freedom, labor, land, and rights.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of postwar southern labor arrangements. White landowners usually controlled land, freed families farmed for crop shares, supplies were bought on credit, and debt often remained at year’s end. Which argument about Reconstruction is best supported?

choices.A: Legal freedom did not automatically produce economic independence.
choices.B: Reconstruction immediately ended all racial and class hierarchy in the rural South.
choices.C: Sharecropping allowed most freedpeople to become wealthy landowners by 1870.
choices.D: Postwar labor systems were unrelated to slavery’s legacy.

questions.correctAnswerText: Legal freedom did not automatically produce economic independence.
questions.explanation: The table shows that emancipation did not eliminate debt, dependency, or white control over land.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Secondary-source interpretation. A historian argues that Gilded Age urban reform was shaped not only by poverty and immigration but also by environmental problems created by dense settlement, horse waste, industrial smoke, unsafe water, and inadequate sewage. Which evidence would best support this interpretation?

choices.A: City health reports linking disease outbreaks to contaminated water and poor sanitation
choices.B: Diplomatic correspondence about the Jay Treaty
choices.C: Records of cotton production in the Deep South
choices.D: Congressional debates over the Missouri Compromise

questions.correctAnswerText: City health reports linking disease outbreaks to contaminated water and poor sanitation
questions.explanation: Such reports directly support the claim that sanitation and environmental conditions shaped urban reform.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Secondary-source interpretation. A historian argues that Gilded Age urban reform was shaped by environmental problems created by dense settlement, horse waste, industrial smoke, unsafe water, and inadequate sewage. Which broader development most directly contributed to these problems?

choices.A: The decline of cities after Reconstruction
choices.B: Rapid industrialization, population growth, and uneven municipal infrastructure
choices.C: The end of immigration to industrial centers
choices.D: The disappearance of factories from urban areas

questions.correctAnswerText: Rapid industrialization, population growth, and uneven municipal infrastructure
questions.explanation: Industrial growth and urban crowding created pollution, waste, water, and sewage problems.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Secondary-source interpretation. A historian argues that Gilded Age urban reform was shaped by environmental problems created by dense settlement, smoke, unsafe water, and inadequate sewage. Which policy response was most closely related to such concerns?

choices.A: The creation of city sanitation departments, water systems, and public health regulations
choices.B: The abolition of all city governments
choices.C: The immediate privatization of all streets and sewers
choices.D: The end of public-health inspection in immigrant neighborhoods

questions.correctAnswerText: The creation of city sanitation departments, water systems, and public health regulations
questions.explanation: Urban environmental problems encouraged municipal public-health and infrastructure reforms.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Secondary-source interpretation. A historian argues that Gilded Age urban reform was shaped by dense settlement, unsafe water, smoke, and inadequate sewage. Which later reform tradition shared the idea that government should address social problems created by industrial life?

choices.A: Progressive Era municipal reform and public-health activism
choices.B: The policy of salutary neglect
choices.C: The constitutional debate over ratification
choices.D: The first colonial assemblies in Virginia

questions.correctAnswerText: Progressive Era municipal reform and public-health activism
questions.explanation: Progressive reformers built on concerns about urban sanitation, health, and government responsibility.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated Social Gospel sermon excerpt, 1890s, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. “The city cannot be saved soul by soul while children breathe factory smoke and families crowd in rooms without light. Christian duty must reach the wage, the tenement, and the council chamber.” The excerpt’s purpose is best explained by which movement?

choices.A: The Social Gospel movement linking Protestant ethics to social reform
choices.B: The First Great Awakening’s revival preaching in the 1740s
choices.C: The Lost Cause movement after Reconstruction
choices.D: The New Right movement of the late twentieth century

questions.correctAnswerText: The Social Gospel movement linking Protestant ethics to social reform
questions.explanation: The sermon applies Christian duty to wages, tenements, factories, and city government.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated Social Gospel sermon excerpt, 1890s, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The speaker says Christian duty must reach “the wage, the tenement, and the council chamber.” Which claim is best supported?

choices.A: Some religious reformers connected moral responsibility to labor conditions, housing, and politics.
choices.B: Social Gospel reformers rejected urban reform.
choices.C: Protestant leaders avoided public issues after the Civil War.
choices.D: Social Gospel reformers focused only on missionary work overseas.

questions.correctAnswerText: Some religious reformers connected moral responsibility to labor conditions, housing, and politics.
questions.explanation: The sermon’s references to wages, tenements, and council chambers connect religion to social reform.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated Social Gospel sermon excerpt, 1890s, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The sermon links factory smoke, tenement housing, wages, and city government to Christian duty. Which argument about late nineteenth-century reform is best supported?

choices.A: Reformers increasingly treated industrial poverty as a moral and public problem, not only a private failure.
choices.B: Reformers agreed that government should never address urban conditions.
choices.C: Religious language disappeared from reform movements after 1865.
choices.D: Social reform was limited to rural farming communities.

questions.correctAnswerText: Reformers increasingly treated industrial poverty as a moral and public problem, not only a private failure.
questions.explanation: The sermon frames urban industrial problems as requiring moral and civic action.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described political cartoon, 1919. A frightened figure labeled “Public Fear” points at shadows labeled “Bolshevism,” “labor strike,” and “immigrant radical.” A government official holds a net labeled “raids,” while civil liberties papers lie on the floor. Which conclusion is best supported by the cartoon?

choices.A: Postwar fear of radicalism encouraged repression of dissent and immigrant activists.
choices.B: The Red Scare expanded civil liberties for suspected radicals.
choices.C: Labor strikes disappeared after World War I.
choices.D: Anti-Communist fears had no relationship to immigration.

questions.correctAnswerText: Postwar fear of radicalism encouraged repression of dissent and immigrant activists.
questions.explanation: The cartoon links public fear, Bolshevism, strikes, immigrants, raids, and weakened civil liberties.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described political cartoon, 1919. A figure labeled “Public Fear” points at shadows labeled “Bolshevism,” “labor strike,” and “immigrant radical.” A government official holds a net labeled “raids,” while civil liberties papers lie on the floor. The cartoon’s historical situation is best explained by which context?

choices.A: The First Red Scare after World War I and the Russian Revolution
choices.B: The debate over the Missouri Compromise
choices.C: The Nullification Crisis
choices.D: The emergence of the Great Society

questions.correctAnswerText: The First Red Scare after World War I and the Russian Revolution
questions.explanation: The cartoon reflects fears of radicalism after World War I and the Bolshevik Revolution.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described political cartoon, 1919. The cartoon links Bolshevism, labor strikes, immigrant radicals, government raids, and damaged civil liberties papers. Which broader social pattern helped produce the fear shown?

choices.A: Wartime nationalism, labor unrest, nativism, and anxiety about revolutionary movements
choices.B: The disappearance of immigration after 1900
choices.C: National agreement that labor unions should control industry
choices.D: Complete public rejection of government surveillance

questions.correctAnswerText: Wartime nationalism, labor unrest, nativism, and anxiety about revolutionary movements
questions.explanation: These pressures combined to produce the First Red Scare.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described political cartoon, 1919. Public fear points at “Bolshevism,” “labor strike,” and “immigrant radical,” while a government official holds a net labeled “raids.” Which later development reflected a similar pattern of domestic anti-Communist suspicion?

choices.A: McCarthy-era investigations and loyalty concerns during the Cold War
choices.B: The First Great Awakening
choices.C: The creation of the Erie Canal
choices.D: The Oregon boundary negotiation

questions.correctAnswerText: McCarthy-era investigations and loyalty concerns during the Cold War
questions.explanation: The McCarthy era repeated patterns of anti-Communist suspicion, investigations, and civil-liberties concerns.

questions.prompt:
Stimulus: Plain-text table of 1920s consumer economy.
Indicator | 1920s pattern
Installment buying | Expanded for automobiles, radios, and appliances
Advertising | Became more national and sophisticated
Automobile ownership | Increased substantially
Stock speculation | Increased among middle-class investors
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Consumer credit, advertising, and mass production helped reshape everyday economic life.
choices.B: Americans rejected automobiles and radios in the 1920s.
choices.C: Advertising declined as consumer goods expanded.
choices.D: Stock speculation had no place in the 1920s economy.

questions.correctAnswerText: Consumer credit, advertising, and mass production helped reshape everyday economic life.
questions.explanation: The table shows installment buying, advertising, automobiles, and speculation shaping the 1920s economy.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of 1920s consumer economy: installment buying expanded for automobiles, radios, and appliances; advertising became more national and sophisticated; automobile ownership increased; stock speculation increased. Which cultural development was most directly connected to these patterns?

choices.A: The decline of mass popular culture
choices.B: The spread of national consumer culture through advertising, radio, and automobiles
choices.C: The disappearance of urban entertainment
choices.D: The end of middle-class interest in new technologies

questions.correctAnswerText: The spread of national consumer culture through advertising, radio, and automobiles
questions.explanation: Advertising, radios, cars, and consumer goods expanded national mass culture.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of 1920s consumer economy: installment buying, national advertising, automobile ownership, and stock speculation all increased. Which later development exposed weaknesses in the economic culture shown?

choices.A: The Great Depression, which revealed risks tied to speculation, credit, and uneven prosperity
choices.B: The American Revolution, which ended British taxation
choices.C: The Mexican-American War, which expanded U.S. territory
choices.D: The Civil Rights Act of 1964, which banned public segregation

questions.correctAnswerText: The Great Depression, which revealed risks tied to speculation, credit, and uneven prosperity
questions.explanation: Speculation, credit, and uneven prosperity contributed to vulnerabilities exposed by the Depression.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of 1920s consumer economy: installment buying expanded, advertising became more national, automobile ownership increased, and stock speculation spread. Which argument is best supported?

choices.A: The prosperity of the 1920s was shaped by new consumption patterns but included financial vulnerabilities.
choices.B: The 1920s economy was entirely rural and noncommercial.
choices.C: Consumer culture reduced all class differences.
choices.D: The federal government banned installment buying during the decade.

questions.correctAnswerText: The prosperity of the 1920s was shaped by new consumption patterns but included financial vulnerabilities.
questions.explanation: The table supports both consumer expansion and risks from credit and speculation.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Secondary-source interpretation. A historian argues that the postwar years combined rising wages, unionized industrial employment, federal housing and education benefits, and expanding consumer goods, but that access to this prosperity was uneven by race, gender, and region. Which evidence would best support this interpretation?

choices.A: GI Bill benefits, rising home ownership, union wages, and discriminatory lending practices
choices.B: The Stamp Act and colonial nonimportation agreements
choices.C: The Dred Scott decision and secession conventions
choices.D: The First Red Scare and Palmer Raids

questions.correctAnswerText: GI Bill benefits, rising home ownership, union wages, and discriminatory lending practices
questions.explanation: These examples show postwar opportunity alongside unequal access.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Secondary-source interpretation. A historian argues that postwar prosperity was shaped by wages, unions, federal benefits, housing, education, and consumer goods, but access was uneven by race, gender, and region. Which broader context helped create this pattern?

choices.A: Economic growth after World War II combined with persistent discrimination and unequal access to federal programs.
choices.B: The collapse of all unions after 1945
choices.C: The disappearance of federal housing policy
choices.D: The immediate end of racial inequality after World War II

questions.correctAnswerText: Economic growth after World War II combined with persistent discrimination and unequal access to federal programs.
questions.explanation: Postwar prosperity was real but unevenly distributed by race, gender, and region.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Secondary-source interpretation. A historian argues that postwar prosperity involved rising wages, federal benefits, housing, education, and consumer goods, but access was uneven by race, gender, and region. Which migration and settlement pattern was most closely connected to these conditions?

choices.A: Expansion of suburbs alongside continuing inequalities in metropolitan housing access
choices.B: Collapse of all metropolitan growth after 1945
choices.C: Return of most families to subsistence farms
choices.D: End of regional migration to the Sun Belt

questions.correctAnswerText: Expansion of suburbs alongside continuing inequalities in metropolitan housing access
questions.explanation: Federal benefits and rising incomes supported suburbanization, while discrimination limited access for many groups.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Secondary-source interpretation. A historian argues that the postwar years combined rising wages, unionized industrial employment, federal housing and education benefits, and expanding consumer goods, but that access to this prosperity was uneven by race, gender, and region. Which argument is best supported?

choices.A: Postwar prosperity expanded opportunity for many Americans while reinforcing important inequalities.
choices.B: Postwar economic growth reached every group equally.
choices.C: Federal policy had no relationship to postwar living standards.
choices.D: Consumer goods disappeared from American life after 1945.

questions.correctAnswerText: Postwar prosperity expanded opportunity for many Americans while reinforcing important inequalities.
questions.explanation: The interpretation emphasizes both growth and unequal access.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described poster from a women’s liberation march, early 1970s. The poster shows a woman holding a briefcase in one hand and a child’s lunchbox in the other. Text reads, “Equal pay, equal choice, equal respect.” Which historical situation is most directly reflected?

choices.A: Women’s liberation activism challenging workplace inequality, gender roles, and reproductive restrictions
choices.B: The campaign for the Fifteenth Amendment
choices.C: The creation of common schools in the 1840s
choices.D: The First Great Awakening

questions.correctAnswerText: Women’s liberation activism challenging workplace inequality, gender roles, and reproductive restrictions
questions.explanation: The poster’s language of equal pay, choice, and respect reflects women’s liberation activism.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described poster from a women’s liberation march, early 1970s. The poster shows a woman holding a briefcase and a child’s lunchbox, with the text “Equal pay, equal choice, equal respect.” Which claim is best supported by the poster?

choices.A: Women’s rights activists linked economic equality to family roles and personal autonomy.
choices.B: Women’s liberation activism focused only on foreign policy.
choices.C: Feminist activists rejected workplace equality as a goal.
choices.D: Gender roles were no longer debated after World War II.

questions.correctAnswerText: Women’s rights activists linked economic equality to family roles and personal autonomy.
questions.explanation: The briefcase, lunchbox, and slogan connect paid work, family responsibility, and autonomy.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described poster from a women’s liberation march, early 1970s. The poster reads “Equal pay, equal choice, equal respect” and shows symbols of paid work and family responsibility. Which policy debate was most closely connected to this activism?

choices.A: Debates over the Equal Rights Amendment and laws against sex discrimination
choices.B: Debates over the Proclamation of 1763
choices.C: Debates over the Mexican Cession
choices.D: Debates over the Chinese Exclusion Act

questions.correctAnswerText: Debates over the Equal Rights Amendment and laws against sex discrimination
questions.explanation: Women’s liberation activism was closely tied to ERA debates and legal challenges to sex discrimination.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated campaign flyer, 1980s, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. “Families, entrepreneurs, and taxpayers deserve a government that rewards work, respects faith, restrains spending, and rebuilds strength abroad.” Which political movement is most directly reflected in the flyer?

choices.A: Modern conservatism
choices.B: Antebellum abolitionism
choices.C: Populism
choices.D: Progressivism

questions.correctAnswerText: Modern conservatism
questions.explanation: The flyer’s emphasis on work, faith, lower spending, entrepreneurship, and military strength reflects modern conservatism.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated campaign flyer, 1980s, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The flyer praises work, faith, lower spending, entrepreneurship, and military strength. Which broader context helped this message gain influence?

choices.A: Reaction against liberal social programs, cultural change, inflation, and perceived weakness abroad
choices.B: The collapse of all religious political activism after 1960
choices.C: Universal support for expanding federal welfare spending in the 1980s
choices.D: The disappearance of Cold War foreign-policy concerns

questions.correctAnswerText: Reaction against liberal social programs, cultural change, inflation, and perceived weakness abroad
questions.explanation: These conditions helped conservative politics gain influence in the late twentieth century.

questions.prompt:
Stimulus: Plain-text table of digital access and economic change.
Pattern | Late 20th to early 21st century trend
Internet access | Expanded rapidly but unevenly by income and location
High-skill technology jobs | Grew in several metropolitan regions
Manufacturing communities | Faced job losses in many areas
Online services | Became more important for education, work, and commerce
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Digital technology expanded opportunity while also creating or reinforcing economic inequalities.
choices.B: Internet access spread evenly to all communities at the same rate.
choices.C: Manufacturing job losses had no regional impact.
choices.D: Online services remained unrelated to education and commerce.

questions.correctAnswerText: Digital technology expanded opportunity while also creating or reinforcing economic inequalities.
questions.explanation: The table shows expanded internet access and technology jobs alongside uneven access and manufacturing job losses.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of digital access and economic change: internet access expanded rapidly but unevenly by income and location; high-skill technology jobs grew in several metropolitan regions; many manufacturing communities faced job losses; online services became more important for education, work, and commerce. Which argument about recent U.S. history is best supported?

choices.A: Technological change has reshaped opportunity, regional economies, and access to public and private services.
choices.B: Digital technology ended regional inequality.
choices.C: Deindustrialization had no connection to modern economic change.
choices.D: Online access became less important after 2000.

questions.correctAnswerText: Technological change has reshaped opportunity, regional economies, and access to public and private services.
questions.explanation: The table connects digital access, regional job growth, deindustrialization, and online services.
